title: The Trump Administration's Threat to Scientific Research - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Tabarrok warns that the Trump administration's rewrite of the Regulation for Federal Financial Assistance would centralize and politicize US science funding, destroying the fragile decentralized equilibrium that underpins American scientific leadership.

themes.theme: Centralization threat to US science funding

insights.claim: The Trump administration's rewrite of the Regulation for Federal Financial Assistance would give the President and political appointees discretion to cancel funding or target grant recipients for virtually any reason with little recourse.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A structural shift toward politicized grantmaking raises tail risk for federally funded research institutions, biotech pipelines, and university endowments dependent on NIH/NSF flows.
insights.quote: give the president and his political appointees discretion to cancel funding or target recipients for virtually any reason—with little opportunity for recourse
insights.locator: Grayson Logue writing at The Dispatch

insights.claim: Tabarrok argues America's historically successful decentralized system of science funding is a fragile exception, and that centralized control is the global default because it serves whoever holds power.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Frames decentralized science as a durable but destroyable competitive advantage; its erosion is a long-horizon negative for US innovation leadership.
insights.quote: Decentralization is the fragile exception—a historically unusual achievement that is easier to destroy than rebuild.
insights.locator: As I wrote in my original post

insights.claim: Tabarrok's real worry is not any single overreach but the loss of an equilibrium in which science funding stayed decentralized and culture-war material remained a tiny fraction of the whole.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: An equilibrium shift, unlike a reversible policy, implies persistent regime change in how US research is governed, hard to price and slow to reverse.
insights.locator: It may be that the Trump administration has pushed too far

insights.claim: Independent commentators across the spectrum, including Dan Drezner and Noah Smith alongside the AAAS, are sounding strong alarms, with Drezner saying the rule is what he would write if trying to ruin American scientific leadership.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Cross-ideological consensus that the change is catastrophic signals elevated, non-partisan concern worth monitoring as a policy risk to the research sector.
insights.quote: if I was trying to ruin American leadership in scientific research this is pretty much the kind of rule I would write
